1	package reseq
2	
3	import (
4		"net/netip"
5		"sync"
6		"time"
7	)
8	
9	// Clock is the injected time source. Production wires a monotonic wall clock
10	// (SystemClock); tests wire a hand-advanced fake so ordering, window, and
11	// timeout behaviour are deterministic with no real sleeps. It is intentionally
12	// structurally identical to telemetry.Clock so the same fake can drive both.
13	type Clock interface {
14		Now() time.Time
15	}
16	
17	// SystemClock is the production Clock backed by the monotonic wall clock.
18	type SystemClock struct{}
19	
20	// Now returns the current monotonic time.
21	func (SystemClock) Now() time.Time { return time.Now() }
22	
23	// Discontinuity/resync guard tuning. A DATA frame is UNAUTHENTICATED by design
24	// (internal/frame/frame.go: "DATA/PARITY forgeable by design"), so a garbage
25	// datagram decoding as KindData (p ~ 1/256) delivers a uniformly-random uint64
26	// OuterSeq, and a genuine peer PROCESS RESTART resets the peer's outerSeq counter
27	// to 1. Neither may be trusted to move the release point arbitrarily.
28	const (
29		// resyncFactor (K) bounds how far AHEAD of the release point a single
30		// unauthenticated frame is trusted to advance the window as a plausible loss
31		// burst. Under bounded cross-path reorder a legitimate frame sits within one
32		// window of next; a forward jump up to K windows is still treated as a loss
33		// burst (the sound bounded-memory window-advance handles it in O(window)), but
34		// a jump of K windows or more is SUSPECT: a single such frame never advances
35		// next. K=4 leaves generous headroom above the 1-window reorder bound while
36		// capping the release point a single trusted frame can skip to (K-1) windows.
37		resyncFactor = 4
38	
39		// resyncCorroborate (C) is how many out-of-band frames carrying DISTINCT seqs
40		// that mutually span less than one window must be observed before the release
41		// point is re-pinned. A genuine peer restart (1,2,3,...) or a consistent
42		// long-outage forward jump emits connected, distinct seqs that trivially
43		// corroborate. A repeated identical seq does NOT advance the count: a single
44		// junk or forged datagram re-delivered — a network duplicate, or a forger
45		// replaying one datagram — contributes only ONE distinct seq, so it can never
46		// self-corroborate. Corroboration therefore requires C INDEPENDENT junk seqs to
47		// land mutually within one window; each is independent in 2^64, so that occurs
48		// with probability ~(window/2^64)^(C-1) ~ 1e-32 for C=3 and window=2048 — junk
49		// never triggers a resync, while a real discontinuity resyncs after losing at
50		// most C-1 frames. Any in-band (near-current) frame resets the run, so ordinary
51		// traffic interspersed with rare junk never accumulates a false corroboration.
52		resyncCorroborate = 3
53	)
54	
55	// Item is one released inner datagram plus the outer source address of the DATA
56	// frame that carried it. The source travels with the payload so the multipath
57	// Bind can pin its single virtual endpoint to the FIRST delivered frame's
58	// source even when that frame was buffered and released out of arrival order.
59	type Item struct {
60		Payload []byte
61		Src     netip.AddrPort
62	}
63	
64	// slot is one ring cell. A live cell (occupied) holds exactly one buffered DATA
65	// frame whose OuterSeq maps to this index; seq is retained so a stale cell (an
66	// already-drained seq that shares the index modulo window) is never mistaken for
67	// a live one.
68	type slot struct {
69		seq      uint64
70		src      netip.AddrPort
71		payload  []byte
72		occupied bool
73	}
74	
75	// Resequencer is a bounded-window, timeout-based receive resequencing buffer. It
76	// consumes DATA frames tagged with the multipath codec's OWN outer sequence
77	// number (frame.Data.OuterSeq — never WireGuard's inner counter) arriving out of
78	// order across paths, and releases the decoded inner datagrams in strictly
79	// ascending outer-seq order before they reach the WireGuard engine. This absorbs
80	// multipath reorder entirely in the outer layer so WG's RFC 6479 anti-replay
81	// filter (window 8128, see docs/p0-findings.md §6) only ever sees monotonically
82	// increasing inner counters and never drops a legitimately-delivered packet.
83	//
84	// Guarantees (see the package tests):
85	//   - Ordering: released frames are strictly ascending in outer-seq. A frame
86	//     whose seq is below the release point is dropped, never delivered late, so
87	//     delivery is monotonic and WG sees no replay-window regression.
88	//   - Exactly-once: duplicates (same seq re-observed before or after release) are
89	//     dropped; every deliverable frame is released exactly once.
90	//   - Bounded memory: at most `window` frames are buffered. A frame at or beyond
91	//     next+window forces the window to advance (releasing/skipping the tail),
92	//     so an adversarial reorder/loss trace cannot grow the buffer without bound.
93	//   - Timeout progress: when the head-of-line seq is missing, buffered frames
94	//     ahead of the gap are held at most `timeout`; then the gap is skipped
95	//     (treated as lost) and the run released, rather than stalling forever. Each
96	//     distinct head-of-line gap gets its OWN full timeout.
97	//   - Bounded compute: every Observe runs in O(window). No frame — however wild
98	//     its (unauthenticated, possibly forged) seq — can make the release point
99	//     advance one seq at a time across a gap of up to 2^64 under the mutex.
100	//   - Discontinuity resilience: an unauthenticated frame whose seq lies farther
101	//     than resyncFactor*window from next never moves the release point on its own.
102	//     A genuine peer restart or long outage — a run of frames carrying DISTINCT
103	//     seqs that mutually fall within one window — re-pins (resyncs) the release
104	//     point within a bounded number of frames; uniformly-random junk seqs, and a
105	//     single junk/forged seq re-delivered any number of times, do not.
106	//
107	// Concurrency: every method is guarded by one mutex, so the shared instance is
108	// safe for the multipath Bind's per-path receive goroutines. The mutex is held
109	// only for in-memory bookkeeping — never across a syscall — so it does not
110	// perturb the Bind's lock-free virtual-endpoint fast path or its
111	// syscall-outside-mutex send discipline.
112	//
113	// FEC seam (T24): Observe is the sole ingestion point and is keyed purely by
114	// outer-seq. An FEC decoder inserted on the receive path recovers missing DATA
115	// frames from PARITY and feeds each recovered frame's ORIGINAL outer-seq through
116	// Observe, identically to a natively-received frame — so FEC recovery slots in
117	// BEFORE resequencing with no change to this type.
118	type Resequencer struct {
119		window  uint64
120		timeout time.Duration
121		clock   Clock
122	
123		mu      sync.Mutex
124		started bool   // false until the first Observe pins the initial next
125		next    uint64 // lowest outer-seq not yet released
126		ring    []slot // len == window; indexed by seq % window
127		buf     int    // number of occupied slots
128	
129		ready    []Item // FIFO of released items awaiting Pop
130		readyPos int    // read cursor into ready (front of the FIFO)
131	
132		// Head-of-line timeout: when next is a gap but frames ahead are buffered,
133		// waiting is armed with a deadline measured from when the gap first formed.
134		waiting  bool
135		deadline time.Time
136	
137		// Discontinuity/resync guard: the current run of out-of-band (suspect) frames.
138		// resyncSeqs holds the DISTINCT suspect seqs seen in the run (a repeated
139		// identical seq does not advance corroboration); [resyncLo, resyncHi] is the
140		// run's seq span. When resyncSeqs reaches resyncCorroborate distinct seqs the
141		// run is deemed a real discontinuity and the release point re-pins (see
142		// tryResync). It holds at most resyncCorroborate seqs, so memory stays bounded.
143		resyncSeqs []uint64
144		resyncLo   uint64
145		resyncHi   uint64
146	
147		// Pending low-anchor re-baseline (peer restart, T119). After RebaselineToLow the
148		// release point is UNPINNED but NOT re-anchored on the next arbitrary frame:
149		// pendingLow keeps `next` held at pendingLowAnchor (the pre-rebaseline release
150		// point) so a stale HIGH-seq straggler still draining from the OLD boot is
151		// SUSPECT-dropped, and `next` re-anchors ONLY when a genuine restarted-stream
152		// low-seq — a frame more than one window BELOW pendingLowAnchor — arrives. This
153		// closes the D36 re-pin race the plain Rebaseline leaves open (plan review R126).
154		pendingLow       bool
155		pendingLowAnchor uint64
156	
157		// Diagnostics (read via the accessors; useful for the bounded-memory asserts).
158		highWater   int    // max occupied slots ever held
159		dropDup     uint64 // frames dropped as duplicates
160		dropLate    uint64 // frames dropped as already-past the release point
161		dropSuspect uint64 // out-of-band frames dropped while (not yet) corroborating
162		skipped     uint64 // seqs skipped (treated lost) by window-advance or timeout
163		releasedN   uint64 // frames released for delivery
164		resyncs     uint64 // release-point re-pins after a corroborated discontinuity
165		rebaselines uint64 // release-point re-baselines forced by a trusted control event (hub failover)
166	}
167	
168	// New returns a resequencer buffering at most window outer-seq positions and
169	// holding a head-of-line-blocked run for at most timeout before skipping the gap.
170	// window must be positive; timeout may be zero (a missing head seq is skipped as
171	// soon as the next Observe/Pop advances the clock past the arrival instant).
172	func New(window uint64, timeout time.Duration, clock Clock) *Resequencer {
173		if window == 0 {
174			panic("reseq: window must be positive")
175		}
176		if clock == nil {
177			panic("reseq: clock must be non-nil")
178		}
179		return &Resequencer{
180			window:  window,
181			timeout: timeout,
182			clock:   clock,
183			ring:    make([]slot, window),
184		}
185	}
186	
187	// Observe ingests one DATA frame's inner payload under its outer-seq. It takes
188	// ownership of payload (the caller must not mutate it afterwards); the multipath
189	// Bind passes the freshly-decoded frame.Data.Payload, which aliases nothing else.
190	// src is the outer source address the frame arrived from. Any frames that become
191	// deliverable are appended to the ready FIFO for Pop.
192	func (r *Resequencer) Observe(seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) {
193		now := r.clock.Now()
194		r.mu.Lock()
195		defer r.mu.Unlock()
196	
197		if !r.started {
198			r.started = true
199			r.next = seq
200		}
201	
202		// Give up on any head-of-line gap whose hold time has elapsed before deciding
203		// where this frame lands.
204		r.expire(now)
205	
206		// Classify the frame against the release window and the discontinuity guard.
207		// admit adjusts next (window-advance or resync) when warranted and reports
208		// whether the frame now lands inside [next, next+window) and should be placed.
209		if !r.admit(seq) {
210			return
211		}
212	
213		cell := &r.ring[seq%r.window]
214		if cell.occupied && cell.seq == seq {
215			r.dropDup++
216			return
217		}
218		cell.seq = seq
219		cell.src = src
220		cell.payload = payload
221		cell.occupied = true
222		r.buf++
223		if r.buf > r.highWater {
224			r.highWater = r.buf
225		}
226	
227		r.drain()
228		r.arm(now)
229	}
230	
231	// ObserveRecovered ingests a payload the FEC decoder reconstructed from parity (T24).
232	// Unlike Observe it NEVER moves or re-pins the release point: a recovered frame is a
233	// REPAIR for a past gap, so if it has already fallen below the release point it is
234	// dropped as too-late, and it can neither window-advance nor corroborate a resync — so
235	// FEC recovery can never CAUSE the burst loss it exists to prevent (a late batch of K
236	// recovered seqs below `next` must not be able to dump the live buffer via a backward
237	// resync). It returns whether the frame was PLACED (landed at or above the release
238	// point and will be delivered in order); a dropped-late recovered frame returns false,
239	// which the datapath uses to keep the /metrics recovered counter honest — counting
240	// frames actually delivered ahead of the release point, not merely reconstructed.
241	func (r *Resequencer) ObserveRecovered(seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) bool {
242		now := r.clock.Now()
243		r.mu.Lock()
244		defer r.mu.Unlock()
245	
246		if !r.started {
247			r.started = true
248			r.next = seq
249		}
250		r.expire(now)
251	
252		// The placement window is EXACTLY [next, next+window). A recovered frame below next
253		// is too late (drop, and — crucially — never feed the resync guard). One at or beyond
254		// next+window would require a window-advance we refuse to perform for a reconstructed
255		// frame, since that would skip live seqs on the strength of a repair. Neither branch
256		// touches next or the corroboration run.
257		if seq < r.next {
258			if r.next-seq > r.window {
259				r.dropSuspect++
260			} else {
261				r.dropLate++
262			}
263			return false
264		}
265		if seq-r.next >= r.window {
266			r.dropLate++
267			return false
268		}
269	
270		cell := &r.ring[seq%r.window]
271		if cell.occupied && cell.seq == seq {
272			r.dropDup++
273			return false // already buffered (a received frame or a prior recovery)
274		}
275		cell.seq = seq
276		cell.src = src
277		cell.payload = payload
278		cell.occupied = true
279		r.buf++
280		if r.buf > r.highWater {
281			r.highWater = r.buf
282		}
283	
284		r.drain()
285		r.arm(now)
286		return true
287	}
288	
289	// admit classifies seq against the release window and the discontinuity guard and
290	// decides whether the frame should be buffered. It returns true when seq now lies
291	// inside [next, next+window) and should be placed; false (having bumped the
292	// appropriate drop counter) when the frame is dropped. It may advance next by a
293	// bounded amount on a plausible loss burst, or re-pin next on a corroborated
294	// discontinuity. Caller holds r.mu.
295	func (r *Resequencer) admit(seq uint64) bool {
296		if r.pendingLow {
297			// A low-anchor re-baseline (peer restart, T119) is in effect: re-anchor the
298			// release point ONLY on the genuine restarted-stream low-seq — a frame more
299			// than one window BELOW the pre-rebaseline release point. A stale HIGH-seq
300			// straggler still draining from the OLD boot (at or near the old release
301			// point) is an ordinary SUSPECT-drop and must NOT re-pin `next` high, so
302			// recovery is not blocked once-per-epoch (defect D36 re-pin race). The
303			// seq < anchor guard is load-bearing: without it a straggler at or above the
304			// anchor would underflow the unsigned subtraction and spuriously re-anchor.
305			if seq < r.pendingLowAnchor && r.pendingLowAnchor-seq > r.window {
306				r.pendingLow = false
307				r.resyncReset()
308				r.next = seq
309				return true
310			}
311			r.dropSuspect++
312			return false
313		}
314		if seq < r.next {
315			// Below the release point. A frame more than one window below next is
316			// impossibly late under bounded reorder — treat it as SUSPECT (a peer
317			// restart resets outerSeq to 1, so its frames land here). A frame within a
318			// window below next is an ordinary straggler/duplicate.
319			if r.next-seq > r.window {
320				if r.tryResync(seq) {
321					return true
322				}
323				r.dropSuspect++
324				return false
325			}
326			r.resyncReset() // near-current traffic: not a discontinuity
327			r.dropLate++
328			return false
329		}
330		// seq >= next.
331		if seq-r.next >= resyncFactor*r.window {
332			// Too far ahead to be a plausible loss burst — SUSPECT (garbage-decoded or
333			// forged high seq). A single such frame must not advance next.
334			if r.tryResync(seq) {
335				return true
336			}
337			r.dropSuspect++
338			return false
339		}
340		// In-window, or a moderate (plausible loss-burst) forward jump: legitimate
341		// near-current traffic, so any in-progress discontinuity run is broken.
342		r.resyncReset()
343		if seq-r.next >= r.window {
344			// Beyond the window: advance the release point so the frame fits at the top
345			// of the window, releasing buffered frames below the new base in order and
346			// skipping the (assumed lost) gaps. This is what bounds memory.
347			r.advanceTo(seq - r.window + 1)
348		}
349		return true
350	}
351	
352	// Pop returns the next in-order released inner datagram, or ok=false when none is
353	// ready. It also advances the head-of-line timeout, so a receive goroutine that
354	// only drains still makes timeout progress.
355	func (r *Resequencer) Pop() (Item, bool) {
356		now := r.clock.Now()
357		r.mu.Lock()
358		defer r.mu.Unlock()
359	
360		r.expire(now)
361	
362		if r.readyPos >= len(r.ready) {
363			return Item{}, false
364		}
365		it := r.ready[r.readyPos]
366		r.ready[r.readyPos] = Item{} // release the payload reference
367		r.readyPos++
368		if r.readyPos >= len(r.ready) {
369			// FIFO drained: reset to reuse the backing array, keeping it bounded.
370			r.ready = r.ready[:0]
371			r.readyPos = 0
372		}
373		return it, true
374	}
375	
376	// drain releases the contiguous run starting at next into the ready FIFO. Caller
377	// holds r.mu.
378	func (r *Resequencer) drain() {
379		for {
380			cell := &r.ring[r.next%r.window]
381			if !cell.occupied || cell.seq != r.next {
382				return
383			}
384			r.release(cell)
385			r.next++
386		}
387	}
388	
389	// advanceTo moves next up to target, releasing any occupied cell it passes (in
390	// ascending order) and counting the empty positions as skipped (lost) seqs. It is
391	// O(window), NEVER O(target-next): all occupied cells live in [next, next+window),
392	// so once next reaches next+window the remaining gap is provably empty and is
393	// closed by arithmetic rather than iterated. Caller holds r.mu; target must be
394	// > next.
395	func (r *Resequencer) advanceTo(target uint64) {
396		// Only [next, next+window) can hold occupied cells; iterate at most that far.
397		limit := target
398		if limit > r.next+r.window {
399			limit = r.next + r.window
400		}
401		for r.next < limit {
402			cell := &r.ring[r.next%r.window]
403			if cell.occupied && cell.seq == r.next {
404				r.release(cell)
405			} else {
406				r.skipped++
407			}
408			r.next++
409		}
410		if r.next < target {
411			// The remaining gap [next, target) is entirely empty — no occupied cell can
412			// live a full window ahead of the old release point. Close it by arithmetic.
413			r.skipped += target - r.next
414			r.next = target
415		}
416	}
417	
418	// release moves one occupied cell's payload into the ready FIFO and clears it.
419	// Caller holds r.mu.
420	func (r *Resequencer) release(cell *slot) {
421		r.ready = append(r.ready, Item{Payload: cell.payload, Src: cell.src})
422		cell.occupied = false
423		cell.payload = nil
424		r.buf--
425		r.releasedN++
426	}
427	
428	// expire skips a head-of-line gap whose hold time has elapsed: it jumps next to
429	// the smallest buffered seq (treating the intervening seqs as lost) and releases
430	// the now-contiguous run. Caller holds r.mu.
431	func (r *Resequencer) expire(now time.Time) {
432		if !r.waiting || now.Before(r.deadline) {
433			return
434		}
435		minSeq, ok := r.smallestBuffered()
436		if !ok {
437			r.waiting = false
438			return
439		}
440		// Skip the lost gap [next, minSeq), then release from minSeq onward.
441		for r.next < minSeq {
442			r.skipped++
443			r.next++
444		}
445		r.drain()
446		// Clear the armed state BEFORE re-arming so that a distinct SECOND gap exposed
447		// by this release gets its own full timeout rather than inheriting this gap's
448		// already-elapsed deadline (which would skip it with ~zero hold).
449		r.waiting = false
450		r.arm(now)
451	}
452	
453	// arm (re)evaluates the head-of-line timeout after a change. When next is a gap
454	// but buffered frames sit ahead of it, it starts the hold clock (only if not
455	// already running, so the deadline measures from when the gap first formed).
456	// Otherwise it disarms. Caller holds r.mu.
457	func (r *Resequencer) arm(now time.Time) {
458		cell := &r.ring[r.next%r.window]
459		headPresent := cell.occupied && cell.seq == r.next
460		if !headPresent && r.buf > 0 {
461			if !r.waiting {
462				r.waiting = true
463				r.deadline = now.Add(r.timeout)
464			}
465		} else {
466			r.waiting = false
467		}
468	}
469	
470	// tryResync feeds one out-of-band (suspect) seq into the discontinuity guard. It
471	// re-pins the release point ONLY after resyncCorroborate DISTINCT suspect seqs
472	// whose values mutually span less than one window (a genuine peer restart or long
473	// outage emits connected, distinct seqs that corroborate; uniformly-random junk
474	// seqs, each independent in 2^64, do not, and a single junk/forged seq re-delivered
475	// contributes only ONE distinct value so it can never self-corroborate). It
476	// returns true and performs the resync when the corroboration threshold is met —
477	// re-pinning next to the triggering seq so the caller places it as the new head
478	// and delivery resumes immediately, without a phantom gap or an extra timeout —
479	// and false while still collecting or when the seq fails to corroborate the
480	// current run. Caller holds r.mu.
481	func (r *Resequencer) tryResync(seq uint64) bool {
482		if len(r.resyncSeqs) == 0 || r.spanExceeds(seq) {
483			// Start (or restart) the run at this seq: it does not corroborate the
484			// current run, so it becomes the anchor of a fresh one.
485			r.resyncLo, r.resyncHi = seq, seq
486			r.resyncSeqs = append(r.resyncSeqs[:0], seq)
487			return false
488		}
489		if r.runContains(seq) {
490			// A repeated identical seq does NOT advance corroboration: a lone junk or
491			// forged datagram re-delivered must not count as independent corroboration.
492			return false
493		}
494		if seq < r.resyncLo {
495			r.resyncLo = seq
496		}
497		if seq > r.resyncHi {
498			r.resyncHi = seq
499		}
500		r.resyncSeqs = append(r.resyncSeqs, seq)
501		if len(r.resyncSeqs) < resyncCorroborate {
502			return false
503		}
504		// Corroborated discontinuity: re-pin to the triggering seq and resume delivery.
505		r.resync(seq)
506		return true
507	}
508	
509	// runContains reports whether seq is already one of the distinct suspect seqs in
510	// the current corroboration run. The run holds at most resyncCorroborate seqs, so
511	// this scan is O(C). Caller holds r.mu.
512	func (r *Resequencer) runContains(seq uint64) bool {
513		for _, s := range r.resyncSeqs {
514			if s == seq {
515				return true
516			}
517		}
518		return false
519	}
520	
521	// spanExceeds reports whether adding seq to the current corroboration run would
522	// make its seq span reach or exceed one window (i.e. seq does not mutually fall
523	// within one window of the run so far). Caller holds r.mu.
524	func (r *Resequencer) spanExceeds(seq uint64) bool {
525		lo, hi := r.resyncLo, r.resyncHi
526		if seq < lo {
527			lo = seq
528		}
529		if seq > hi {
530			hi = seq
531		}
532		return hi-lo >= r.window
533	}
534	
535	// resync re-pins the release point to base after a confirmed discontinuity,
536	// discarding all buffered frames (they belong to the pre-discontinuity stream) in
537	// O(window). The already-released FIFO is untouched — those were legitimate prior
538	// deliveries. Caller holds r.mu.
539	func (r *Resequencer) resync(base uint64) {
540		for i := range r.ring {
541			r.ring[i] = slot{}
542		}
543		r.buf = 0
544		r.next = base
545		r.waiting = false
546		r.resyncReset()
547		r.resyncs++
548	}
549	
550	// resyncReset abandons the current corroboration run. Caller holds r.mu.
551	func (r *Resequencer) resyncReset() {
552		r.resyncSeqs = r.resyncSeqs[:0]
553		r.resyncLo = 0
554		r.resyncHi = 0
555	}
556	
557	// Rebaseline discards the buffered (pre-switch) frames and UNPINS the release
558	// point so the NEXT Observe re-anchors `next` to that frame's outer-seq, exactly as
559	// if the stream had just started. It exists for the edge-side HUB FAILOVER (T57): a
560	// concentrator switch changes the DATA-frame SENDER, and the standby is a separate
561	// process whose outer-seq restarts near 1 — far below the release point the prior
562	// hub's high-rate stream advanced `next` to. The unauthenticated tryResync path
563	// cannot rescue this in time: it needs resyncCorroborate (3) distinct low seqs
564	// within one window, but a freshly re-handshaking standby emits only ~1 DATA frame
565	// (the handshake response) per RekeyTimeout, so corroboration falls outside the
566	// failover window and the response is dropped as SUSPECT — the tunnel never
567	// re-establishes (defect D32). Unlike a DATA frame (forgeable by design, hence the
568	// corroboration guard), THIS re-pin is driven by a TRUSTED control event — the
569	// operator-configured ordered-endpoint switch, above the unauthenticated wire — so
570	// re-anchoring on the next single frame is sound. Buffered frames (from the dead
571	// prior hub) are discarded in O(window); the already-released FIFO of prior
572	// legitimate deliveries is untouched. Idempotent and safe before the first Observe
573	// (started stays false). Takes r.mu; the caller must NOT hold it.
574	func (r *Resequencer) Rebaseline() {
575		r.mu.Lock()
576		defer r.mu.Unlock()
577		for i := range r.ring {
578			r.ring[i] = slot{}
579		}
580		r.buf = 0
581		r.started = false
582		r.waiting = false
583		// A full unpin SUPERSEDES any pending low-anchor re-baseline (T119). If a peer
584		// restart had armed pendingLow (via RebaselineToLow) and the restarted low-seq
585		// init had not yet re-anchored `next`, a subsequent D32 hub failover must NOT
586		// leave the stale pendingLow gate in force: admit would keep classifying every
587		// fail-back frame against the now-stale pendingLowAnchor and blackhole the
588		// standby's stream, violating this method's documented "next Observe re-anchors
589		// next" postcondition. Clearing it restores the plain unpin-and-trust-next path.
590		r.pendingLow = false
591		r.resyncReset()
592		r.rebaselines++
593	}
594	
595	// RebaselineToLow re-baselines the release point for a PEER RESTART (T119) using a
596	// LOW-ANCHOR rule the plain Rebaseline lacks. Like Rebaseline it discards the
597	// buffered pre-restart frames in O(window) and leaves the already-released FIFO
598	// untouched, but instead of unpinning `next` and trusting the NEXT frame to
599	// re-anchor, it PINS a pending low-anchor at the current release point: `next`
600	// re-anchors ONLY when a frame more than one window BELOW that point arrives — the
601	// genuine restarted-stream low-seq (the wrapped WG init, outer-seq ~1). Any stale
602	// HIGH-seq straggler still draining from the OLD boot lands at or near the old
603	// release point and is SUSPECT-dropped, so it can never re-pin `next` high and
604	// block recovery (the D36 re-pin race under the saturation precondition that a
605	// plain Rebaseline — which re-anchors on the first frame, high straggler included —
606	// cannot survive; plan review R126). Idempotent across repeated restart signals: a
607	// second call while a re-anchor is still pending keeps the ORIGINAL (high) anchor.
608	// Safe before the first Observe (an UNSTARTED ring needs no re-anchor — the first
609	// Observe pins next normally).
610	//
611	// The low-anchor gate is armed ONLY when the pre-rebaseline release point is high
612	// enough for it to be satisfiable: the re-anchor predicate is
613	// `seq < pendingLowAnchor && pendingLowAnchor - seq > window`, and the restarted
614	// sender's first DATA is outer-seq ~1, so it can only re-anchor when
615	// pendingLowAnchor >= window+2 (i.e. next > window+1). For a SMALL anchor
616	// (next <= window+1) the predicate is UNSATISFIABLE for any low seq — nothing would
617	// ever clear pendingLow and every subsequent frame would be SUSPECT-dropped forever
618	// (a permanent blackhole; the pre-T119 code self-heals here via resync corroboration,
619	// so pinning would be a regression). This is the realistic light-traffic / early-
620	// restart / crash-loop case. So when the anchor is small we fall back to a PLAIN
621	// unpin (started=false), re-anchoring on the next frame exactly as the D32
622	// Rebaseline does — bounded, self-healing loss instead of a blackhole. Takes r.mu;
623	// the caller must NOT hold it.
624	func (r *Resequencer) RebaselineToLow() {
625		r.mu.Lock()
626		defer r.mu.Unlock()
627		for i := range r.ring {
628			r.ring[i] = slot{}
629		}
630		r.buf = 0
631		r.waiting = false
632		r.resyncReset()
633		r.rebaselines++
634		switch {
635		case r.pendingLow:
636			// Already pending from an earlier restart signal this epoch: keep the ORIGINAL
637			// (high) anchor — idempotent under repeated restart signals for the same epoch.
638		case r.started && r.next > r.window+1:
639			// The release point is high enough for the low-anchor gate to be satisfiable:
640			// hold `next` at the pre-rebaseline release point so stale-high stragglers
641			// SUSPECT-drop until the restarted low-seq re-anchors it.
642			r.pendingLow = true
643			r.pendingLowAnchor = r.next
644		default:
645			// UNSTARTED ring, or a small release point (next <= window+1) where a low-anchor
646			// gate would be UNSATISFIABLE and permanently blackhole the restarted stream.
647			// Fall back to a plain unpin: the next Observe re-anchors next, self-healing
648			// exactly as the D32 hub-failover Rebaseline does. On an unstarted ring this is
649			// a no-op (started is already false).
650			r.started = false
651		}
652	}
653	
654	// smallestBuffered scans the ring for the lowest occupied seq at or above next.
655	// All occupied seqs lie in [next, next+window), so this is bounded by window and
656	// runs only on the (rare) timeout path. Caller holds r.mu.
657	func (r *Resequencer) smallestBuffered() (uint64, bool) {
658		best := uint64(0)
659		found := false
660		for i := range r.ring {
661			c := &r.ring[i]
662			if c.occupied && c.seq >= r.next && (!found || c.seq < best) {
663				best = c.seq
664				found = true
665			}
666		}
667		return best, found
668	}
669	
670	// Buffered reports the number of frames currently held (not yet released). It is
671	// always <= window, which the bounded-memory tests assert.
672	func (r *Resequencer) Buffered() int {
673		r.mu.Lock()
674		defer r.mu.Unlock()
675		return r.buf
676	}
677	
678	// Pending reports the number of released-but-not-yet-popped items in the FIFO.
679	func (r *Resequencer) Pending() int {
680		r.mu.Lock()
681		defer r.mu.Unlock()
682		return len(r.ready) - r.readyPos
683	}
684	
685	// HighWater reports the maximum number of frames ever buffered simultaneously.
686	func (r *Resequencer) HighWater() int {
687		r.mu.Lock()
688		defer r.mu.Unlock()
689		return r.highWater
690	}
691	
692	// Stats is a snapshot of the resequencer's cumulative counters.
693	type Stats struct {
694		Released       uint64 // frames released for delivery
695		DroppedDup     uint64 // frames dropped as duplicates
696		DroppedOld     uint64 // frames dropped as already-past the release point
697		DroppedSuspect uint64 // out-of-band frames dropped while (not yet) corroborating
698		Skipped        uint64 // seqs skipped (lost) by window-advance or timeout
699		Resyncs        uint64 // release-point re-pins after a corroborated discontinuity
700		Rebaselines    uint64 // release-point re-baselines forced by a trusted control event (hub failover)
701	}
702	
703	// Stats returns a snapshot of the cumulative counters.
704	func (r *Resequencer) Stats() Stats {
705		r.mu.Lock()
706		defer r.mu.Unlock()
707		return Stats{

--- a/internal/reseq/reseq.go
+++ b/internal/reseq/reseq.go
@@ -580,6 +580,14 @@ func (r *Resequencer) Rebaseline() {
 	r.buf = 0
 	r.started = false
 	r.waiting = false
+	// A full unpin SUPERSEDES any pending low-anchor re-baseline (T119). If a peer
+	// restart had armed pendingLow (via RebaselineToLow) and the restarted low-seq
+	// init had not yet re-anchored `next`, a subsequent D32 hub failover must NOT
+	// leave the stale pendingLow gate in force: admit would keep classifying every
+	// fail-back frame against the now-stale pendingLowAnchor and blackhole the
+	// standby's stream, violating this method's documented "next Observe re-anchors
+	// next" postcondition. Clearing it restores the plain unpin-and-trust-next path.
+	r.pendingLow = false
 	r.resyncReset()
 	r.rebaselines++
 }
@@ -598,7 +606,21 @@ func (r *Resequencer) Rebaseline() {
 // cannot survive; plan review R126). Idempotent across repeated restart signals: a
 // second call while a re-anchor is still pending keeps the ORIGINAL (high) anchor.
 // Safe before the first Observe (an UNSTARTED ring needs no re-anchor — the first
-// Observe pins next normally). Takes r.mu; the caller must NOT hold it.
+// Observe pins next normally).
+//
+// The low-anchor gate is armed ONLY when the pre-rebaseline release point is high
+// enough for it to be satisfiable: the re-anchor predicate is
+// `seq < pendingLowAnchor && pendingLowAnchor - seq > window`, and the restarted
+// sender's first DATA is outer-seq ~1, so it can only re-anchor when
+// pendingLowAnchor >= window+2 (i.e. next > window+1). For a SMALL anchor
+// (next <= window+1) the predicate is UNSATISFIABLE for any low seq — nothing would
+// ever clear pendingLow and every subsequent frame would be SUSPECT-dropped forever
+// (a permanent blackhole; the pre-T119 code self-heals here via resync corroboration,
+// so pinning would be a regression). This is the realistic light-traffic / early-
+// restart / crash-loop case. So when the anchor is small we fall back to a PLAIN
+// unpin (started=false), re-anchoring on the next frame exactly as the D32
+// Rebaseline does — bounded, self-healing loss instead of a blackhole. Takes r.mu;
+// the caller must NOT hold it.
 func (r *Resequencer) RebaselineToLow() {
 	r.mu.Lock()
 	defer r.mu.Unlock()
@@ -609,13 +631,23 @@ func (r *Resequencer) RebaselineToLow() {
 	r.waiting = false
 	r.resyncReset()
 	r.rebaselines++
-	if r.started && !r.pendingLow {
-		// Enter pending low-anchor mode, holding `next` at the pre-rebaseline release
-		// point so stale-high stragglers SUSPECT-drop until the restarted low-seq
-		// re-anchors it. An already-pending re-baseline keeps its original anchor —
-		// idempotent under repeated restart signals for the same epoch.
+	switch {
+	case r.pendingLow:
+		// Already pending from an earlier restart signal this epoch: keep the ORIGINAL
+		// (high) anchor — idempotent under repeated restart signals for the same epoch.
+	case r.started && r.next > r.window+1:
+		// The release point is high enough for the low-anchor gate to be satisfiable:
+		// hold `next` at the pre-rebaseline release point so stale-high stragglers
+		// SUSPECT-drop until the restarted low-seq re-anchors it.
 		r.pendingLow = true
 		r.pendingLowAnchor = r.next
+	default:
+		// UNSTARTED ring, or a small release point (next <= window+1) where a low-anchor
+		// gate would be UNSATISFIABLE and permanently blackhole the restarted stream.
+		// Fall back to a plain unpin: the next Observe re-anchors next, self-healing
+		// exactly as the D32 hub-failover Rebaseline does. On an unstarted ring this is
+		// a no-op (started is already false).
+		r.started = false
 	}
 }

### Session summary
- **Did:** reviewed task T119 round 2 (diff e8cbc55..82d9793; merge-base verified == current main, no stale base). Re-ran build/vet/tests + race in the worktree; ran the two new regression tests against round-1 branch `implement/T119` (both fail there with the exact blackhole signatures: 0/8 and 0/100 delivered); wrote and executed two adversarial probes against round-2.
- **Achieved:** verdict **disapprove**; 2 criticisms / 0 questions / 1 out-of-scope defect filed.
- **Discovered:** FIX 1 and FIX 2 genuinely resolved (small-anchor restart delivers; RebaselineToLow→Rebaseline delivers; D32 tests green; `go build`, `go vet`, `go test ./...`, `go test -race ./internal/bind/...` all pass; per-peer scoping/witness isolation/stale-high race/same-epoch-no-rebaseline all asserted through the real receive path). BUT: (A) empirical probe — anchor=window+2 armed, new-boot seq 1 lost → **0/499 delivered, DroppedSuspect=499, permanent** (the pendingLow branch bypasses the resync corroboration self-heal and has no bound/timeout; repeated restart signals keep the original anchor); (B) empirical probe — `ObserveRecovered` (production-wired FEC repair, multipath.go:1889) bypasses the pendingLow gate and places an old-boot frame while armed; the admit() re-anchor does not clear the ring, so the stale cell drove the head-of-line timeout to jump `next` 2→210 (Skipped:208), suspect-dropped new-boot frames 2–3, and delivered a stale old-boot frame into the new stream.
- **Issues:** none blocking a confident verdict.

If that single wrapped-init frame is lost — loss under saturation IS the D36 premise — every subsequent new-boot frame fails `anchor-seq > window` and is suspect-dropped forever: repro delivered 0/499 with DroppedSuspect=499. The pendingLow branch of admit() (reseq.go:296-313) bypasses tryResync corroboration entirely (the pre-T119 self-heal), has no drop-count cap or timeout fallback, and the idempotency branch (reseq.go:635) retains the original anchor across repeated restart signals, so nothing ever clears the gate.
